food_subgroup: Cantonese-style thick white rice congee with century egg (preserved egg) and seasoned minced pork

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for century egg and minced pork congee. No fabricated URI assigned.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice porridge (congee) — closest parent; specific century egg and pork variant not individually coded in FoodEx2
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00HU covers rice porridge generically. No specific facet code for century egg topping exists; closest_parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of century egg and minced pork congee as served in Singaporean Chinese restaurants and hawker centres, weighing approximately 350g.

nutrient_source.title: Century Egg and Minced Pork Congee — Health Promotion Board Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) — Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database for century egg and minced pork congee. Values represent the composite dish as prepared and served in Singapore. The input record cites foodo.sg which is the HPB-affiliated food database portal.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice Porridge (Congee) — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to century egg and minced pork congee variant
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of ~78 (high) is based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for plain rice congee/porridge. The addition of century egg and minced pork does not significantly alter the GI of the congee base, as these toppings contribute primarily protein and fat rather than rapidly digestible carbohydrate. GL per 350g serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (78 × 28.0) / 100 = 21.84, rounded to 21.8. Carbohydrate per serving = 8.0 g/100g × 350g = 28.0g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content — 192mg/100g; 672mg per 350g bowl (34% of 2,000mg daily RDI)
hypertension.guidance: Century egg and minced pork congee is one of the highest-sodium congee preparations available; the century egg alone contributes approximately 450mg sodium per half egg from alkaline preservation salts, and patients with hypertension should strictly limit or avoid this dish, or request preparation with reduced century egg and no added soy sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (~78) and medium-high GL (~21.8 per 350g bowl) from the rice congee base
type2_diabetes.guidance: The high GI of the congee base raises postprandial blood glucose; however, the 14g of protein per 350g bowl from minced pork and century egg provides a moderate glycemic-moderating effect, and patients with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in controlled portions, monitor postprandial glucose, and avoid additional soy sauce or condiments that increase sodium load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (672mg/bowl) and moderate cholesterol (203mg/bowl at 58mg/100g) from century egg and minced pork
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (672mg per bowl) and moderate dietary cholesterol from century egg are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; patients with established cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional servings, request reduced century egg, and avoid added condiments to keep total sodium within daily limits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lead content in century egg — modern Singapore-sold products use NaOH process, within safe limits
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Modern century eggs sold in Singapore are produced using sodium hydroxide rather than traditional lead-lime methods and are within safe lead limits per HPB guidelines; however, patients with rheumatoid arthritis consuming century egg at high frequency should ensure products are sourced from Singapore-regulated suppliers to minimise heavy metal exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Century egg and minced pork congee (皮蛋瘦肉粥, Pí Dàn Shòu Ròu Zhōu) is the quintessential Cantonese congee, ubiquitous across Singapore in Chinese restaurants, dim sum establishments, hawker centres, and hospital meal services. It is prepared by slow-cooking white rice in a large volume of water or stock until the grains break down into a silky, thick porridge, then topping with sliced century egg (preserved duck egg with a dark translucent white and creamy green-grey yolk) and seasoned minced pork, finished with sesame oil, soy sauce, ginger, and spring onion. The texture is smooth and warming; the flavour is savoury and umami-rich with a distinctive alkaline note from the century egg. Nutritionally, a 350g bowl provides approximately 245 kcal, 14g protein, 7g fat, 28g carbohydrate, and 672mg sodium. The dish is nutritionally distinct from plain congee primarily due to its sodium content: the century egg contributes approximately 450mg sodium per half egg from the alkaline preservation process, making this one of the highest-sodium congee preparations. The GI is high (~78) based on the rice congee base, with a glycemic load of approximately 21.8 per bowl. Clinically, the primary concern is sodium for patients with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. For patients with Type 2 Diabetes, the protein from pork and egg provides moderate glycemic buffering. Century eggs sold in Singapore use modern NaOH-based production methods that comply with HPB lead-safety guidelines.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database, which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The food is an exact match in the HPB database. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for rice congee, which is the standard methodology for congee variants. All mandatory multilingual names are present.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite congee dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this prepared dish is not individually reported in the HPB database. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source — no trans fats in this traditional preparation). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no vitamin C-containing ingredients in this preparation). All other nutrient values are as reported by HPB Singapore. GI of 78 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain rice congee; the addition of century egg and minced pork does not materially alter the GI of the congee base. GL per 350g serving = (78 × 28.0) / 100 = 21.84. Sodium note: the 192mg/100g sodium value reflects the composite dish including century egg preservation salts and pork seasoning. Century eggs sold in Singapore comply with HPB lead-safety guidelines using NaOH-based production. The input record listed two portions with is_default=true for both; the canonical serving size of 350g (verified source) is used as the default serving.
